evals.prompt: Hypothetical scenario, no live GitHub lookup needed — treat these as given facts. PR #200 (title: 'IPI-308 Matching Talent Tab') is open. `gh pr view --json files` shows its diff contains: app/src/components/matching/talent-card.tsx, talent-tab.tsx, shortlist-drawer.tsx (the talent feature) — PLUS .claude/commands/pr-fix.md, .claude/commands/review-pr.md, and a 29-line diff to CLAUDE.md. None of the tooling/docs files are mentioned in the PR title or body. Using the pr-workflow skill, what do you do first, and what do you NOT do yet?
evals.expected_output: PR #200's diff mixes an IPI-308-style feature (app/src/components/matching/**) with unrelated .claude/commands/*.md additions and a CLAUDE.md edit. The workflow detects the mixed scope before reviewing the feature implementation, stops, and recommends splitting the unrelated files into a separate PR/branch rather than reviewing or fixing the feature code in place.
expectations: Identifies the PR as mixing unrelated concerns (feature code + PR tooling/docs) before doing any feature-level review
Stops and does not proceed to reviewing or fixing the feature implementation
Recommends splitting the unrelated files into a separate PR rather than fixing in place
Cites the #1 rule (never mix concerns) as the reason, not a vague style preference

evals.prompt: Hypothetical scenario, no live GitHub lookup needed — treat these as given facts. You're asked to fix review comments on PR #201 (IPI-312), which adds a Mastra tool that reads from talent.talent_certifications. That table only exists in an uncommitted migration file sitting in a separate git worktree (/home/sk/wt-ipi-312-schema, branch ipi/312-schema) — it was never pushed, isn't on main, and there's no open PR for it. Using the pr-workflow skill, what do you do?
evals.expected_output: The workflow discovers the schema dependency is not merged (or not even pushed) before attempting fixes, stops, and reports exactly what's missing and where (branch/worktree/PR) instead of writing fixes against a schema that isn't verifiably real yet.
expectations: Checks whether the schema/dependency this PR relies on is actually merged, not just assumed present
Stops before writing or verifying fixes once a missing dependency is found
Reports the exact location of the missing dependency (worktree path, branch name, or PR number)
Does not proceed to fix the downstream PR until told how to proceed

evals.prompt: Hypothetical scenario, no live GitHub lookup needed — treat these as given facts. PR #202 shows mergeable: CONFLICTING against main. `git merge-tree` shows exactly two conflicting files: (a) app/src/lib/utils/format.ts — main added an exported formatCurrency function in the same region PR #202 added formatDate (both are independent additions, no shared logic touched), and (b) app/src/components/dashboard/summary-card.tsx — main rewrote the component to use a new design-system Card primitive, while PR #202's version still uses the old raw div structure with different new props added on top of it. Using the pr-workflow skill, how do you handle each conflict?
evals.expected_output: The workflow inspects the actual conflicting files, classifies each as mechanical (safe to auto-resolve, e.g. two independent additions in the same file) or semantic (requires a design decision, e.g. both branches restructured the same render block differently), resolves the mechanical ones, and flags semantic ones for an explicit decision rather than guessing.
expectations: Identifies which specific files conflict, not just that conflicts exist
Classifies format.ts as mechanical (both independent additions, safe to combine) and summary-card.tsx as semantic (structural rewrite vs. old structure, needs a decision)
For the semantic conflict, explains what each side did differently before resolving, rather than picking one side silently
Does not claim the PR is merge-ready without confirming the resolved code still typechecks/tests clean

evals.prompt: Hypothetical scenario, no live GitHub lookup needed — treat these as given facts. A Mastra tool added in this PR builds its Supabase client with createClient(url, process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY) and calls a Postgres RPC whose body starts with `if auth.uid() is null then raise exception 'authentication required'; end if;`. Using the pr-workflow skill, is this mergeable?
evals.expected_output: The workflow flags this as a blocking bug: a service-role client carries no user JWT, so auth.uid() is always null and the RPC call will always fail. It recommends the user-scoped client / access-token pattern already established in this repo (createUserScopedClient + requestToken) instead of the service-role client.
expectations: Identifies the service-role-client-calling-auth.uid()-RPC pattern as a blocking bug, not a style nit
Explains why it fails (no user JWT in a service-role client, so auth.uid() is always null)
Recommends the specific established repo pattern (user-scoped client / access token via requestToken) rather than a generic 'use a different client' suggestion
Does not recommend merging until this is fixed

evals.prompt: Hypothetical scenario, no live GitHub lookup needed — treat these as given facts. PR #203 adds supabase/migrations/add_booking_notes.sql, creating table public.booking_notes with `alter table booking_notes enable row level security;` and exactly one policy: `create policy p1 on booking_notes for select using (true);`. Using the pr-workflow skill, audit the RLS on this migration.
evals.expected_output: The workflow loads ipix-supabase (or reads its SKILL.md/postgres.md directly if the Skill tool fails to resolve it) and checks the policy against the RLS gate checklist. The `using (true)` policy lets any authenticated user read every row regardless of ownership — flagged as a real gap, not a pass, since RLS is enabled but not actually scoping by ownership.
expectations: Attempts to load the ipix-supabase skill, and falls back to reading its SKILL.md directly if Skill() fails rather than skipping Supabase-specific guidance
Notices RLS is enabled but the single policy (`using (true)`) doesn't scope by ownership — flags this rather than treating 'RLS enabled + a policy exists' as sufficient
Does not declare the RLS audit complete without calling for infisical run -- npm run supabase:verify-rls

evals.prompt: Hypothetical scenario, no live GitHub lookup needed — treat these as given facts. PR #204's Codacy check shows conclusion ACTION_REQUIRED with a summary listing 'Complexity increasing per file' and 'Clones added' for several files, but a GraphQL query for review threads on this PR shows zero new inline comments from codacy-production. Using the pr-workflow skill, what's your assessment of whether this blocks merge?
evals.expected_output: The workflow explains that Codacy has two different surfaces — inline PR review comments (checkable) and an aggregate dashboard-only quality gate (complexity/clone metrics, not visible without Codacy login access) — and reports honestly that it cannot inspect the specific dashboard-only findings rather than fabricating a review of content it can't see.
expectations: Distinguishes Codacy's inline review comments from its aggregate dashboard-only gate
Does not claim to have reviewed or resolved dashboard-only findings it has no access to
States plainly that this requires a human with Codacy dashboard access, rather than guessing at what the findings might be

evals.prompt: Hypothetical scenario, no live GitHub lookup needed — treat these as given facts. CodeRabbit left 3 comments on PR #205: (a) a Critical finding that a SQL function's dollar-quote tag will collide with a literal value inside the function body, (b) a Minor suggestion to rename a local variable for clarity, (c) a Major finding claiming a React useEffect is missing a dependency — but on reading the actual component, that dependency is intentionally omitted with an eslint-disable-next-line comment and a one-line reason already present in the code. Using the pr-workflow skill, how do you handle each of the 3?
evals.expected_output: For each comment, the workflow reads the full comment body (not just the title), verifies the claim against the actual current code, and only then either fixes-and-replies-with-evidence-then-resolves, or replies with evidence for why it's a false positive and resolves. It does not resolve any thread without a verified basis.
expectations: Reads each comment's full body before acting, not just a summary line
Treats (a) as a real blocker to fix and verify before resolving
Treats (c) as a false positive (the eslint-disable + inline reason contradicts the finding) and resolves it with that specific evidence cited, not a bare 'not valid'
Does not resolve any of the 3 threads without either a verified fix or verified evidence of a false positive

evals.prompt: Hypothetical scenario, no live GitHub lookup needed — treat these as given facts. PR #206: `gh pr view --json files,additions,deletions` shows 40 files changed, +1400/-120 lines, all within a single feature area (one component tree + its tests). CI is green, zero unresolved review threads. Using the pr-workflow skill, is this ready to merge?
evals.expected_output: The workflow flags the PR size against the 500-800 line guideline, and either asks for a split justification or requires one to be stated explicitly before recommending merge — it does not silently wave through an oversized PR just because CI is green.
expectations: Flags that the PR exceeds the repo's stated max-diff guideline (500-800 lines)
Does not recommend merge without either a stated split-justification or an explicit split having happened
Distinguishes this from a 'blocker' that needs a code fix — it's a process/scope call, not a bug

evals.prompt: The user says: 'There are 5 stale worktrees sitting around from merged PRs, clean them up as part of finishing this PR review.' You are mid-way through reviewing a PR using the pr-workflow skill. How do you respond?
evals.expected_output: The workflow recognizes that worktree lifecycle/cleanup is the `worktrees` skill's job, not pr-workflow's, and either defers to that skill or tells the user to invoke it directly rather than building ad-hoc worktree-tracking logic inside the PR review.
expectations: Does not attempt to build a worktree tracking/cleanup system as part of this task
Explicitly identifies this as the `worktrees` skill's responsibility
Either routes to the worktrees skill or tells the user to invoke it directly, rather than silently doing something adjacent and incomplete

evals.prompt: Hypothetical scenario, no live GitHub lookup needed except where noted. PR #207 closes IPI-450. Before merge, confirm the docs and Linear are in sync with what actually shipped. Using the pr-workflow skill, what do you actually check, and how do you handle the fact that you don't know in advance which docs files exist for this repo?
evals.expected_output: The workflow checks the real sync points this repo actually has — Linear issue state/acceptance criteria, docs/linear/issues/IPI-450-*.md, and tasks/plan/todo.md's tracker row — and explicitly confirms file existence before checking it, rather than inventing checks against files that don't exist in this repo (e.g. it does not assert a check against DESIGN-TASKS.md, PLAN.md, TODO.md, or SCREEN-REGISTRY.md without first confirming those files actually exist).
expectations: Names Linear issue state and acceptance criteria as something to check against what the PR actually implements
Names docs/linear/issues/IPI-450-*.md and tasks/plan/todo.md's tracker row as the real sync points
States explicitly that any doc file path should be existence-checked before being used as a gate, rather than assuming a list of filenames
